fulltext:
REASONS FOR JUDGMENT

CHRISTIE J:

These financial proceedings are listed for final hearing before me commencing on 1 June 2026.
On 5 December 2025 the Court made orders and notations listing the matter for trial and making provision for the filing of evidence.
As at 5 December 2025 the following items of property (in China) did not have agreed values:
(a) AA Pty Ltd;

(b) BB Pty Ltd;

(c) CC Pty Ltd.

The husband contends that CC Pty Ltd ceased trading in 2017 and as a former 5 per cent shareholder he does not have access to any documents. Implicitly the wife does not accept these contentions.
On 24 December 2025 the wife filed an Application in a Proceeding seeking orders for the engagement of single experts to value the husband’s interests in the three corporations incorporated in China, listed above.
I am told the corporations are engaged in import/export.
In the ordinary course parties are encouraged to, and generally do, agree to the identity of the single expert. In this case the parties are asking the court to adjudicate because they have been unable to agree.
The Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia (Family Law) Rules 2021 (Cth) (“the Rules) provide guidance in respect of the adjudication of the dispute:
(a) Rule 7.05(b) requires the names of experts and evidence they have consented to appointment; and

(b) Rule 7.05(b) requires evidence in respect of fees;

(c) Parties and their representatives must not communicate unilaterally with witnesses except as permitted by the Rules: Rule 7.03(3).

The issues I am required to determine are:
(a) Should a single expert be appointed to value the entity which the husband says he no longer has any interest in?

(b) Do the proposed experts have the requisite expertise?

(c) Have unilateral approaches on behalf of the husband rendered some identified experts inappropriate?

Should a single expert be appointed to value the entity which the husband says he no longer has any interest in?

The husband says he has no present interest in CC Pty Ltd and has not done so since 2017.
The wife says she has not been provided with documents to confirm this position.
It is self evident that if the husband is correct then the parties should not engage an expert to value the husband’s interest in CC Pty Ltd.
There are a few options. The parties engage an expert to provide an opinion based on publicly available information confined in the first instance to the question of ownership. If the husband has any present entitlement, then the valuer appointed proceeds with a valuation. The wife engages an expert to opine on ownership and value of CC Pty Ltd. In that case she bears the costs subject to any successful application for costs. Finally, the husband provides documents which satisfy the wife about the present position. There may be other options which have not been flagged by the evidence which the parties are free to pursue by consent.
I do not accept that the case management orders of 5 December 2025 require valuation of this entity if there is cogent evidence that the husband has not had any interest since 2017. The evidence of the husband said:
... [CC Pty Ltd] is no longer operational and ceased trading in 2017. As a result, it is not possible to carry out a valuation in the same way as is required for [AA Pty Ltd] and [BB Pty Ltd]. The financial records of [CC Pty Ltd] are unavailable to me as I only held a 5% shareholding at the date [CC Pty Ltd] ceased trading.
The correspondence between a proposed valuer and the husband’s lawyers appears on its face to be at odds with the husband’s evidence. It says:
In addition, according to the National Enterprise Credit Information Publicity System, [CC Pty Ltd] is in operation and has no abnormal business operations/administrative penalties/serious illegal and untrustworthy situations. It has normally submitted annual reports to the Industry and Commerce Department for the years 2024 and before, but the annual report information is not publicly declared. Therefore, the financial statements as of 2025/09/30 need to be provided for quotation calculation.

Given the proximity of the final hearing, I propose the following course: the orders will include an order for valuation of the husband’s interest in the CC Pty Ltd entity. The parties can agree to depart from the order in the event that the husband provides the wife with documents which satisfy her that valuation is unnecessary. In due course one or other of the parties may consider a costs application in respect of this discrete valuation issue.

Do the proposed experts have the requisite expertise?

The husband seeks that the Court make an order appointing one of the following as single expert:
(a) DD Ltd;

(b) EE Ltd;

(c) FF Ltd;

(d) PwC (China); and

(e) Deloitte (China).

It is necessary when making an order to appoint a natural person who consents to preparing a report, adopting it on oath/affirmation and being prepared to adhere to the expert rules including, if required, answering questions, and giving oral evidence.
The husband does not identify an individual (unless it is suggested that the person identified as the “contact person” would be the author of any report).
The material in support of the husband’s position does identify one person from PWC, Mr GG. But it is less than clear that he is the proposed single expert.
It is also necessary to identify an individual so that both parties (and the Court) are satisfied that person has the requisite qualifications and expertise.
The husband filed material which included business licences from DD Ltd; EE Ltd; and FF Ltd.
DD Ltd must be excluded because the material filed does not demonstrate that any particular individual has qualifications and expertise to undertake a valuation of a business or corporation.
FF Ltd must be excluded because the material filed does not demonstrate that any particular individual has qualifications and expertise to undertake a valuation of a business or corporation. One person Mr HH is described in a register from 25 April 2024 as being a “certified asset valuer”. It is not clear what that means. All other persons are identified as real estate or land valuers.
There is even less information about the experience and qualifications of unnamed persons at EE Ltd so it too must be excluded.
The evidence addresses, albeit obliquely, the willingness and qualifications of the representatives from Deloitte and PwC.
The wife seeks appointment of:
(a) United Assets & Real Estate Appraisal Co. Ltd;

(b) CHFT Appraisal; or

(c) Colliers business Valuation.

In that regard her application suffers from the same deficit as that of the husband - namely to identify an individual with the requisite qualifications and experience who consents to the appointment.
On the face of the evidence there would be individuals at CHFT, Colliers and United Assets and Real estate Appraisal Co Ltd who do possess the requisite experience, but the application does not name them.
The difference between the firms proposed by the husband and those proposed by the wife is that those proposed by the wife appear to have extensive experience in valuation of corporate and business interests while those proposed by the husband appear to be primarily concerned with valuation of real estate.

Have unilateral approaches on behalf of the husband rendered some identified experts inappropriate?

The husband accepts that unilateral approaches have been made but says the inquiries were limited to determining availability and fee estimates.
It is plain that the husband’s lawyer spoke with representatives of both Deloitte and PWC. It is also clear that an agent/colleague of the husband made approaches to valuation firms in China.
It cannot be known with precision what was said by either the party seeking to engage the expert (or his representative) or the proposed expert. The wife’s concerns or beliefs are not (as the husband’s counsel submitted) evidence of an issue but in the circumstances of this case they are evidence of distrust.
The potential difficulty is two-fold – it raises the spectre that the proposed expert has information which is not documented which may impact upon the valuation exercise; and it also undermines the confidence of the party who has not engaged in the discussion in the impartial status of the single expert. For both these reasons, it is unwise for the court to sanction the selection of a single expert with whom a party has (even inadvertently) made unilateral contact.
Both counsel submitted that the parties’ trust was low. In that circumstance it makes no sense to appoint a person where there has been unilateral contact on behalf of one party.
I accept, as was submitted on behalf of the husband that whoever is ultimately selected will owe a duty to the court. However, I am being called upon to make a decision now.
I accept that if all things were equal, more competitive fees (and in particular proportionate fees) could be a relevant factor. The difficulty here is that while I was taken to some very limited material about the asset and income/profit positions of the two companies in which the husband accepts he has an interest, the wife does not accept the validity of the underlying data. Again, this may be an issue which is relevant to a costs dispute (if any) in due course.
I am not persuaded that the fact that the husband’s inquiries were first in time has any material consequence for this application – given I am being asked to select from a number of different options in circumstances where the parties disagree and all the requirements of the Rules are not met.
I am satisfied that it is appropriate to make an order that the husband provide to the single expert any and all documents requested by the single expert in order to carry out the valuation within 7 days of receipt of any request. This time frame is necessary to ensure the hearing proceeds on the allocated dates.
The conclusion I have reached is that it is necessary for me to make mechanical orders which allow the wife to nominate an identified single expert from those firms she has identified.
